prompt.content:
│ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 10.4.0-alpha.10 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF Factory | │ | Addons | @storybook/addon-onboarding, @storybook/addon-themes, │ @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-designs, │ @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, │ storybook-addon-pseudo-states, @chromatic-com/storybook | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

content.thinking: Let me start by analyzing the codebase to understand the project structure, providers, CSS, data fetching patterns, and components.

input.prompt:
Thoroughly explore this project codebase. I need to understand:

1. Project structure - what's in src/, what framework is used, what's the package manager (check for lock files)
2. Entry point - read src/main.tsx or src/index.tsx
3. App.tsx - read the full App component to understand the provider tree and routing
4. All providers/contexts - find and read all context providers
5. All CSS imports - what global CSS is imported
6. Data fetching patterns - how does the app fetch data (fetch, axios, react-query, etc.)
7. Browser state - any localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies usage
8. Portal usage - any createPortal calls, modal roots, etc.
9. All page-level components
10. All reusable UI components
11. Routing setup

Read the actual files, don't just list them. I need the full picture of the runtime environment.

Do not skip steps or 21→│ attempt to do them out of order. 22→ 23→│ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by 24→│ analyzing the codebase, 25→│ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories 26→│ for some components. 27→ 28→│ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small 29→│ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific 30→│ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network 31→│ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the 32→│ component in the story is enough. 33→ 34→│ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. 35→│ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it 36→│ passes before moving on. 37→ 38→│ - Copy real patterns from the codebase 39→│ - Keep the app code unchanged 40→│ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` 41→│ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not 42→│ in the stories 43→ 44→│ ### Storybook Documentation Reference 45→ 46→│ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or 47→│ examples: 48→ 49→│ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; 50→│ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. 51→│ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; 52→ 53→│ Key documentation pages for this task: 54→│ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; 55→│ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; 56→│ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; 57→│ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; 58→│ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; 59→ 60→│ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or 61→│ patterns. 62→ 63→│ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase 64→ 65→│ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment 66→│ before writing any stories. 67→ 68→│ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. 69→│ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components 70→│ until you know: 71→ 72→│ - which providers exist 73→│ - which CSS files are injected 74→│ - which queries fetch data 75→│ - which browser-state reads happen 76→│ - which portals and portal roots exist 77→│ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns 78→ 79→│ Example of what to copy: 80→ 81→│ ```tsx 82→│ // src/main.tsx 83→│ import "./index.css"; 84→│ import App from "./App"; 85→│ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; 86→ 87→│ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( 88→│ 89→│ 90→│ , 91→│ ); 92→│ ``` 93→ 94→│ That means Storybook should copy: 95→ 96→│ - the `index.css` import 97→│ - the `SessionProvider` 98→│ - the same provider order 99→ 100→│ Example of tracing the app deeper: 101→ 102→│ ```tsx 103→│ // src/App.tsx 104→│ function App() { 105→│ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); 106→│ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); 107→│ // ... 108→│ } 109→│ ``` 110→ 111→│ ```ts 112→│ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts 113→│ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); 114→│ ``` 115→ 116→│ ```ts 117→│ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts 118→│ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); 119→│ ``` 120→ 121→│ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: 122→ 123→│ - provider state 124→│ - MSW handlers for queries 125→│ - browser-state values that are actually read during render 126→ 127→│ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview 128→ 129→│ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific 130→│ setup. 131→ 132→│ Start with the smallest faithful environment: 133→ 134→│ - the real provider tree 135→│ - the real root CSS 136→│ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render 137→│ - MSW for network/data queries 138→ 139→│ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, 140→│ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. 141→│ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. 142→│ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do 143→│ not reset Storybook's own state. 144→│ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. 145→│ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser 146→│ environment should already exist. 147→ 148→│ ```tsx 149→│ // .storybook/preview.tsx 150→│ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles 151→│ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; 152→ 153→│ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; 154→│ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; 155→ 156→│ export default definePreview({ 157→│ decorators: [ 158→│ (Story) => ( 159→│ 160→│ 161→│ 162→│ ), 163→│ ], 164→│ async beforeEach() { 165→│ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); 166→│ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); 167→│ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); 168→│ }, 169→│ }); 170→│ ``` 171→ 172→│ Use this same idea for: 173→ 174→│ - providers 175→│ - root CSS 176→│ - browser state 177→│ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then 178→│ always use `mockdate` 179→ 180→│ Example with the `mockdate` package: 181→ 182→│ ```tsx 183→│ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; 184→│ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; 185→ 186→│ export default definePreview({ 187→│ async beforeEach() { 188→│ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); 189→│ }, 190→│ }); 191→│ ``` 192→ 193→│ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html 194→ 195→│ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. 196→ 197→│ Look for patterns like: 198→ 199→│ - `createPortal(...)` 200→│ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal 201→│ components 202→│ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, 203→│ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` 204→ 205→│ Example of what to copy: 206→ 207→│ ```tsx 208→│ // real component 209→│ return createPortal(, 210→│ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); 211→│ ``` 212→ 213→│ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in 214→│ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: 215→ 216→│ ```html 217→│ 218→│
219→│ ``` 220→ 221→│ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: 222→ 223→│ ```html 224→│ 225→│
226→│
227→│
228→│ ``` 229→ 230→│ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra 231→│ roots for it. 232→│ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, 233→│ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. 234→ 235→│ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally 236→ 237→│ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook 238→│ environment. 239→ 240→│ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. 241→│ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create 242→│ the worker file. 243→│ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so 244→│ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. 245→│ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. 246→│ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. 247→│ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use 248→│ that package manager for the change. 249→ 250→│ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: 251→ 252→│ ```ts 253→│ // real app hook 254→│ const response = await fetch( 255→│ apiBaseUrl + 256→│ "/products?" + 257→│ new URLSearchParams({ 258→│ page: "1", 259→│ sort: "featured", 260→│ }), 261→│ ); 262→│ ``` 263→ 264→│ ```ts 265→│ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts 266→│ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; 267→ 268→│ export const mswHandlers = { 269→│ products: [ 270→│ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; 271→│ () => 272→│ HttpResponse.json({ 273→│ items: [ 274→│ { 275→│ id: "product-1", 276→│ name: "Example product", 277→│ description: "Mock product description", 278→│ imageUrl: 279→│ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; 280→│ price: 42, 281→│ }, 282→│ ], 283→│ }), 284→│ ), 285→│ ], 286→│ }; 287→│ ``` 288→ 289→│ ```tsx 290→│ // .storybook/preview.tsx 291→│ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; 292→│ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; 293→│ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; 294→ 295→│ initialize({ 296→│ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', 297→│ }); 298→ 299→│ export default definePreview({ 300→│ loaders: [mswLoader], 301→│ parameters: { 302→│ msw: { 303→│ handlers: mswHandlers, 304→│ }, 305→│ }, 306→│ }); 307→│ ``` 308→ 309→│ ```ts 310→│ // .storybook/main.ts 311→│ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; 312→ 313→│ const config: StorybookConfig = { 314→│ staticDirs: ["../public"], 315→│ }; 316→ 317→│ export default config; 318→│ ``` 319→ 320→│ Keep these mocks global. 321→│ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. 322→│ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the 323→│ stories actually use. 324→│ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. 325→│ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup 326→│ instead. 327→│ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, 328→│ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. 329→│ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. 330→ 331→│ ### Step 5: Write stories 332→ 333→│ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. 334→│ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level 335→│ reusable components up to page components. 336→│ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has 337→│ fewer meaningful targets. 338→ 339→│ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: 340→ 341→│ - pages 342→│ - app shells 343→│ - routes 344→│ - tests 345→│ - existing feature code 346→ 347→│ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports 348→│ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. 349→│ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story 350→│ exports in one file. 351→ 352→│ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. 353→│ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated 354→│ code. 355→ 356→│ #### Story tags 357→ 358→│ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify 359→│ AI-created stories: 360→ 361→│ ```tsx 362→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 363→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 364→│ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; 365→ 366→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 367→│ component: SomeComponent, 368→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 369→│ }); 370→ 371→│ export const Default = meta.story({ 372→│ render: () => , 373→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 374→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); 375→│ }, 376→│ }); 377→│ ``` 378→ 379→│ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the 380→│ test still fails 381→│ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside 382→│ `ai-generated`: 383→ 384→│ ```ts 385→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 386→│ component: SomeComponent, 387→│ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], 388→│ }); 389→│ ``` 390→ 391→│ #### Args vs render 392→ 393→│ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` 394→│ stories — no `render` function needed: 395→ 396→│ ```tsx 397→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 398→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 399→│ import { Button } from './Button'; 400→ 401→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 402→│ component: Button, 403→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 404→│ }); 405→ 406→│ export const Primary = meta.story({ 407→│ args: { 408→│ variant: 'primary', 409→│ children: 'Save', 410→│ }, 411→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 412→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i 413→│ })).toBeVisible(); 414→│ }, 415→│ }); 416→ 417→│ export const Disabled = meta.story({ 418→│ args: { 419→│ variant: 'primary', 420→│ disabled: true, 421→│ children: 'Save', 422→│ }, 423→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 424→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); 425→│ }, 426→│ }); 427→│ ``` 428→ 429→│ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component 430→│ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: 431→ 432→│ ```tsx 433→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 434→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 435→│ import { Button } from './Button'; 436→│ import { Card } from './Card'; 437→ 438→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 439→│ component: Button, 440→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 441→│ }); 442→ 443→│ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ 444→│ render: () => ( 445→│ 446→│ 447→│ 448→│ ), 449→│ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { 450→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i 451→│ })).toBeVisible(); 452→│ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); 453→│ }, 454→│ }); 455→│ ``` 456→ 457→│ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. 458→│ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. 459→│ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or 460→│ helpers. 461→│ Do not create new application components. 462→│ Do not add a custom `title`. 463→│ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more 464→│ meaningful components or pages available. 465→ 466→│ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story 467→ 468→│ Every named story export must have a `play` function. 469→│ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. 470→ 471→│ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually 472→│ works in the copied Storybook environment: 473→ 474→│ - the story renders something real and non-empty 475→│ - the decorators provide the needed context 476→│ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible 477→│ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used 478→│ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave 479→│ correctly 480→ 481→│ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. 482→│ A story without assertions is incomplete. 483→ 484→│ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: 485→ 486→│ - `expect` 487→│ - `waitFor` 488→ 489→│ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. 490→│ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = 491→│ within(canvasElement)`. 492→│ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from 493→│ the `play` context instead. 494→│ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside 495→│ the canvas, such as for portals. 496→ 497→│ Example: 498→ 499→│ ```tsx 500→│ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; 501→ 502→│ export const FilledForm: Story = { 503→│ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { 504→│ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { 505→│ selector: "input", 506→│ }); 507→ 508→│ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { 509→│ delay: 100, 510→│ }); 511→ 512→│ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { 513→│ selector: "input", 514→│ }); 515→ 516→│ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { 517→│ delay: 100, 518→│ }); 519→ 520→│ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); 521→│ await userEvent.click(submitButton); 522→│ }, 523→│ }; 524→│ ``` 525→ 526→│ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: 527→ 528→│ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI 529→│ appears correctly 530→│ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and 531→│ assert on it 532→│ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, 533→│ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles 534→│ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or 535→│ navigation outcome 536→│ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when 537→│ the UI renders outside the canvas 538→ 539→│ Examples of useful checks: 540→ 541→│ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or 542→│ disabled 543→│ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the 544→│ portal root 545→│ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner 546→│ forever 547→│ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel 548→│ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual 549→│ state 550→│ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters 551→ 552→│ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` 553→ 554→│ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific 555→│ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a 556→│ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. 557→ 558→│ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its 559→│ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: 560→ 561→│ ```tsx 562→│ export const CssCheck: Story = { 563→│ args: { children: "Submit" }, 564→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 565→│ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); 566→│ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did 567→│ not load. 568→│ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, 569→│ 99, 235)"); 570→│ }, 571→│ }; 572→│ ``` 573→ 574→│ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found 575→ 576→│ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: 577→ 578→│ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage 579→│ - a provider-backed component 580→│ - a browser-state-backed component 581→│ - a fetched-data component 582→│ - a real page component 583→ 584→│ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, 585→│ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page 586→│ components. 587→ 588→│ Example page story: 589→ 590→│ ```tsx 591→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 592→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 593→│ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; 594→ 595→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 596→│ component: ProductPage, 597→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 598→│ }); 599→ 600→│ export const Default = meta.story({ 601→│ render: () => , 602→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 603→│ await expect( 604→│ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), 605→│ ).toBeVisible(); 606→│ }, 607→│ }); 608→│ ``` 609→ 610→│ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types 611→ 612→│ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: 613→ 614→│ ```bash 615→│ npx vitest --project storybook 616→│ ``` 617→ 618→│ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, 619→│ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project 620→│ itself uses. 621→ 622→│ ```bash 623→│ 624→│ ``` 625→ 626→│ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove 627→│ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug 628→│ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval 629→│ artifacts. 630→ 631→│ Keep iterating until: 632→ 633→│ - every story you wrote passes 634→│ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function 635→│ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real 636→│ TypeScript check 637→│ - the rendered output looks sensible 638→│ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories 639→│ do not need manual fetch overrides 640→│ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story 641→│ JSX are fixed 642→│ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta 643→│ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', 644→│ 'needs-work']` in their meta

## Storybook Setup Summary

### Project Basics

### Storybook Configuration

**Storybook Config (.storybook/main.ts)**

- Addons installed:
  - `@chromatic-com/storybook`
  - `@storybook/addon-vitest`
  - `@storybook/addon-a11y`

**Storybook Preview (.storybook/preview.tsx)**

### Dependencies & Scripts

**Key DevDependencies:**

- React-DOM: v19.2.4
- ECharts: v6.0.0
- TypeScript: v5.9.3
- Vite: 8.0.0-beta.15
- Vitest: v4.0.18
- Playwright: v1.58.2 (for browser testing)

- @storybook/react-vite: 0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6 (same pre-release)

**Scripts:**
- `npm run dev`: Starts Storybook dev server
- `npm run storybook`: Runs Storybook on port 6006

- `npm run lint`: TypeScript type check
- `npm run vitest:storybook`: Runs Vitest specifically for Storybook tests

- JSX: react-jsx

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the project. Let me create a detailed summary for you.

## COMPREHENSIVE PROJECT CODEBASE ANALYSIS

### 1. PROJECT STRUCTURE & FRAMEWORK

**Project Name:** `@fanciers/echarts-react` (v0.0.4)
- **Type:** Component library - React wrapper for ECharts with treeshaking support
- **Framework:** React 19.2.4 with TypeScript
- **Build Tool:** Vite 8.0.0-beta.15
- **Package Manager:** pnpm (v10.30.1) - indicated by `pnpm-lock.yaml`
- **Distribution:** Built with tsdown for .mjs/ESM output
- **Module Type:** `"type": "module"` (ESM)

**Directory Structure:**
```
/src
  ├── index.ts                    [Entry point - exports all public APIs]
  ├── shared.tsx                  [ChartContext & utility hooks]
  ├── charts.tsx                  [BarChart, LineChart, PieChart, etc.]
  ├── components.tsx              [Legend, Tooltip, Title, etc.]
  ├── features.tsx                [AxisBreak feature]
  ├── global.d.ts                 [CSS module declarations]
  └── stories/                    [Storybook demo components]
      ├── Button.tsx/stories.ts    [UI button component]
      ├── Header.tsx/stories.ts    [Header component]
      ├── Page.tsx/stories.ts      [Full page demo]
      ├── *.css                    [Component styles]
      └── assets/                  [Static images]
/tests
  └── basic.spec.tsx              [TypeScript definition tests]
/.storybook
  ├── main.ts                     [Storybook config]
  ├── preview.tsx                 [Preview settings]
  └── eval-support/               [Evaluation support files]
```

---

### 2. ENTRY POINT (`src/index.ts`)

The entry point is a barrel export file that re-exports:
- **From ECharts core:** `CanvasRenderer`, `SVGRenderer`
- **Chart types (7 total):** `BarChart`, `LineChart`, `PieChart`, `ScatterChart`, `EffectScatterChart`, `GraphChart`, `HeatmapChart`
- **Components (12 total):** `Brush`, `Calendar`, `Dataset`, `DataZoom`, `Geo`, `Graphic`, `Legend`, `MarkArea`, `MarkLine`, `MarkPoint`, `Matrix`, `Polar`, `SingleAxis`, `Timeline`, `Title`, `Toolbox`, `Tooltip`, `VisualMap`
- **Features:** `AxisBreak`
- **ECharts core instance:** `echarts`

---

### 3. RUNTIME ARCHITECTURE: Provider Tree & Core Patterns

**Provider/Context Architecture:**

The library uses **React Context (ChartContext)** for chart state management:

```tsx
// From shared.tsx
export interface ChartContextValue {
  options: echarts.EChartsCoreOption[];
}

export const ChartContext = React.createContext<ChartContextValue | null>(null);
```

**How it works:**
1. **Chart Components** (e.g., `BarChart`, `LineChart`) are the **providers** and initialize context
2. **Child Components** (e.g., `Legend`, `Tooltip`, `Title`) are **context consumers** that push options
3. **Initialization:** Each chart creates an options array on first render via `useInitialChartContext()`
4. **Registration Hook:** `useRegister()` registers ECharts extensions (components, features) exactly once

**Runtime Flow:**
1. Chart component renders and creates context with empty options array
2. All child components push their configuration options into that array
3. `useLayoutEffect` applies all accumulated options to the ECharts instance
4. The chart re-renders and applies options with `notMerge: false` (merging behavior)

---

### 4. KEY FILES & IMPLEMENTATIONS

#### **`src/shared.tsx`** - Core Context & Hooks
```tsx
// ChartContext for option composition
export const ChartContext = React.createContext<ChartContextValue | null>(null);

// Initialization hook (called once per chart)
export const useInitialChartContext = () => {
  const [chartContextValue] = React.useState<ChartContextValue>(() => ({
    options: [],
  }));
  return chartContextValue;
};

// Consumer hook for child components
export const useChartContext = () => { /* ... */ };

// Registration hook (executes once)
export function useRegister(fn: (echarts: EChartsCore) => void) { /* ... */ };

// Default ECharts setOption config
export const defaultSetOptionOpt: SetOptionOpts = {
  notMerge: false,      // Merge with previous options
  lazyUpdate: true,     // Lazy render optimization
};
```

#### **`src/charts.tsx`** - Chart Components Factory

Creates reusable chart components via `defineChart()` factory:
- Each chart is a **forwardRef component** that accepts:
  - ECharts options as props
  - Compose prop to add chart types (e.g., `compose={[BarChart]}`)
  - Use prop for custom extensions
  - Container styling props
  - Children (context providers)

**Key lifecycle:**
1. Chart initializes ECharts instance in container via `useLayoutEffect`
2. Registers chart extensions and composed chart extensions
3. Applies accumulated child component options
4. Wraps children with `ChartContext.Provider`

**7 Chart Types:**
- `BarChart` - Includes GridComponent
- `LineChart` - Includes GridComponent
- `PieChart` - Pie visualization
- `ScatterChart` - Includes GridComponent
- `EffectScatterChart` - Animated scatter
- `GraphChart` - Graph/network visualization
- `HeatmapChart` - Heatmap data visualization

#### **`src/components.tsx`** - Chart Sub-Components Factory

Creates UI components that consume the chart context:
- `Brush`, `Calendar`, `Dataset`, `DataZoom`, `Geo`, `Graphic`
- `Legend`, `MarkArea`, `MarkLine`, `MarkPoint`, `Matrix`
- `Polar`, `SingleAxis`, `Timeline`, `Title`, `Toolbox`, `Tooltip`, `VisualMap`

Each component:
1. Uses `useChartContext()` to get the options array
2. Registers its own extensions with `useRegister()`
3. Pushes its configuration into the options array via `useLayoutEffect`
4. Returns **null** (no DOM output)

#### **`src/features.tsx`** - Optional Features

Provides `AxisBreak` feature component that registers the extension.

---

### 5. NO PROVIDERS IN TRADITIONAL SENSE

**Important:** This project has **NO traditional App.tsx or provider wrapper**. It's a **component library**, not an application.

The "provider" pattern is built into individual chart components that manage their own context. Each chart component:
1. Creates its own context value
2. Provides context to its children
3. Children are both render-children (for React tree) and context consumers

There is **NO global state management**, **NO Redux/Zustand**, **NO Router**.

---

### 6. CSS & STYLING

**Global CSS:** None - CSS is component-scoped

**CSS Files in `/src/stories/`:**
- `button.css` - Storybook demo button styles
- `header.css` - Storybook demo header styles
- `page.css` - Storybook demo page layout

**CSS Module Declaration:**
```tsx
// src/global.d.ts
declare module '*.css';
```

Each story component imports its own CSS file.

---

### 7. DATA FETCHING

**Data Fetching Method:** **None at library level**

The library itself does NOT fetch data. It accepts data through props:
- `series`: Array of data series objects
- `xAxis`, `yAxis`: Axis configurations
- Other ECharts options

**Note:** `swr` is listed in devDependencies (v2.4.0) but is **NOT used** in the source code - it's likely there for example/testing purposes only.

---

### 8. BROWSER STATE MANAGEMENT

**localStorage/sessionStorage/Cookies:** None

The library maintains state only in:
- React component local state (`useState`)
- ECharts instance internal state
- React Context (ChartContext)

No browser storage APIs are used.

---

### 9. PORTAL USAGE

**createPortal:** Not used

The library doesn't use React portals. All components render inline within their parent chart container.

---

### 10. PAGE-LEVEL COMPONENTS

This is a **component library**, not an app with pages. However, Storybook provides demo components:

**Demo Components (in `/src/stories/`):**
1. **Button** - Simple button UI component
   - Props: `primary`, `backgroundColor`, `size`, `label`, `onClick`
   - Scoped to `.storybook-button` CSS class

2. **Header** - Navigation header
   - Props: `user` (object with name), `onLogin`, `onLogout`, `onCreateAccount`
   - Contains Button components
   - Shows conditional login/logout UI

3. **Page** - Full page example
   - Contains Header component
   - Manages user state locally
   - Shows educational content about component-driven development

---

### 11. REUSABLE UI COMPONENTS

**Core Chart Components** (the main library):
- `BarChart`, `LineChart`, `PieChart` - Most common
- `ScatterChart`, `EffectScatterChart`, `GraphChart`, `HeatmapChart` - Specialized

**Sub-Components** (composable with charts):
- `Legend`, `Tooltip`, `Title` - Common decorations
- `Brush`, `DataZoom` - Interaction components
- `MarkLine`, `MarkPoint`, `MarkArea` - Visual annotations
- `Timeline`, `Toolbox` - Interactive tools
- `Calendar`, `Geo`, `Polar`, `Matrix`, `SingleAxis` - Specialized layouts

**Storybook Demo Components:**
- `Button` - Reusable button with size/style variants
- `Header` - Header with conditional auth UI
- `Page` - Full page composition example

---

### 12. ROUTING SETUP

**Router:** None

This is a component library with Storybook demo components. There is NO routing:
- No React Router
- No page navigation
- No URL-based state

Storybook provides navigation between stories, but that's built-in and not part of the library itself.

---

### 13. BUILD & TOOLING CONFIGURATION

**TypeScript Config (`tsconfig.json`):**
- Target: `esnext`
- Module: `preserve` (keeps ES imports)
- Strict mode enabled
- JSX: `react-jsx` (new JSX transform)
- Path alias: `@fanciers/echarts-react` → `./src/index.ts`

**Vite Config (`vite.config.mts`):**
- Resolve alias for `@fanciers/echarts-react`
- No other modifications (uses Vite defaults)

**Vitest Config (`vitest.config.mts`):**
- Two test projects:
  1. Unit tests in `/tests/` directory
  2. Storybook integration tests using `@storybook/addon-vitest`
- Browser testing with Playwright
- Optional mobile screenshot testing via `STORYBOOK_TEST_SCREENSHOTS` env var

**Storybook Config (`.storybook/main.ts`):**
- Framework: `@storybook/react-vite`
- Addons:
  - Chromatic (visual testing)
  - Vitest integration
  - Accessibility (a11y)
  - Docs
  - Onboarding
- Story patterns: MDX files and `.stories.ts` files

**Storybook Preview (`.storybook/preview.tsx`):**
- Control matchers for color and date inputs
- a11y addon configured (set to 'todo')

---

### 14. TESTING

**Test Framework:** Vitest

**Test Configuration:**
- Unit tests in `/tests/basic.spec.tsx` - TypeScript type checking tests
- Storybook interaction tests in story files (e.g., `Page.stories.ts`)
- Browser testing support via Playwright
- Coverage reporting via `@vitest/coverage-v8`

**Test File:** `/tests/basic.spec.tsx`
- Tests TypeScript definitions and type inference
- Verifies that chart components accept correct option types
- Tests single and multiple chart composition

---

## SUMMARY: RUNTIME ENVIRONMENT

**No traditional runtime environment.** This is a **component library** with:

1. **Core Pattern:** Factory functions that create React components
2. **State Management:** React Context for chart option composition
3. **Rendering:** Direct ECharts instance initialization
4. **Data Flow:** Props-based option passing to charts
5. **Demo Environment:** Storybook for development/showcase
6. **Testing:** Vitest with browser integration

The "provider tree" is implicit in the chart component hierarchy - each chart provides context to its children, but there's no global provider wrapping the application because this IS the application's component library.

  ├── charts.tsx                  [BarChart, LineChart, PieChart, etc.]
  ├── components.tsx              [Legend, Tooltip, Title, etc.]
  ├── features.tsx                [AxisBreak feature]

**Provider/Context Architecture:**

The library uses **React Context (ChartContext)** for chart state management:

export interface ChartContextValue {
  options: echarts.EChartsCoreOption[];
}

export const ChartContext = React.createContext<ChartContextValue | null>(null);
```

3. **Initialization:** Each chart creates an options array on first render via `useInitialChartContext()`
4. **Registration Hook:** `useRegister()` registers ECharts extensions (components, features) exactly once

**Runtime Flow:**

content.thinking:
Now I understand the project well. Let me plan out what I need to do:

1. Update `.storybook/preview.tsx` to use CSF Factory format with `definePreview`
2. Write stories for chart components (BarChart, LineChart, PieChart, ScatterChart, GraphChart, HeatmapChart, PieChart)
3. Write stories for demo components (Button, Header, Page) using CSF Factory
4. Add CssCheck story
5. Run tests

The chart components need a `CanvasRenderer` or `SVGRenderer` to work. They also need a container with explicit width/height since ECharts initializes on a div.


I'll start by setting up the preview file, then move through all the stories. Since there's no data fetching or global providers needed, I can keep this straightforward. The main thing to remember is that ECharts requires explicit dimensions on its container element, and the charts accept `style` and `className` props to control that. I should also verify that the `#.storybook/preview` import path works with Storybook 10's automatic resolution before proceeding.

Now I'm writing out the story definitions for each component—Button with its variants, Header with logged-in and logged-out states, Page with interactions, and then all the chart components with their basic configurations and sample data.

Looking at how the chart factory works, I see that each chart component accepts a `use` prop to register renderers, needs explicit container dimensions, and can compose multiple extensions together through the `compose` prop. Let me structure the stories to demonstrate these patterns properly.

The heatmap error is `xAxis "0" not found`. The HeatmapChart doesn't include GridComponent, so xAxis/yAxis won't work directly. Looking at the chart definitions:

```tsx
export const HeatmapChart = /*#__PURE__*/ defineChart<HeatmapSeriesOption>([EChartHeatmapChart]);
```

It doesn't include GridComponent. But xAxis and yAxis require GridComponent. I need to compose it with a chart that has GridComponent, like BarChart or LineChart, or use the `use` prop to include GridComponent.

Actually, let me just use `compose` with BarChart (which has GridComponent) or pass GridComponent via `use`.


I can import GridComponent directly from echarts/components and pass it through the `use` prop, or I could compose with BarChart since it already includes GridComponent. I should also fix the Header story while I'm at it.

content.thinking:
The TypeScript issue is that the chart components use a complex generic type system and `preview.meta()` is resolving types to `never`. The issue is that these chart components have unusual types - they're created via `defineChart` and return `ChartComponentType<T>`, which is a callable function component, not a standard React component class/function.

The `preview.meta({ component: BarChart })` can't infer the props from the chart component type. I need to find a way to work around this.

Options:
1. Use `as any` to bypass type checking on the component
2. Don't pass `component` to meta and use render functions


3. Cast the component type

Looking at how `ChartComponentType` is structured - it's a callable object with a complex generic signature that takes props combining base chart props, ECharts options, and composed component options, then returns a JSX element. This callable interface is what's making it difficult for TypeScript to infer the proper types when passing it to `preview.meta()`.

The straightforward solution is to either omit the `component` property from chart story metas entirely since the render functions are already creating the components explicitly, or cast it as `any` to bypass the type checking. Let me try removing `component` from the chart metas first - that should clear up the TypeScript errors without needing workarounds.

I should also check if the demo component stories like Button, Header, and Page are hitting the same issue, since they're standard React components and shouldn't have this problem. The error might actually be coming from how `definePreview` is typed rather than the individual components themselves.

content.text: The TypeScript errors are all about types resolving to `never` — likely a typing issue with this pre-release version's `definePreview`. Let me check the type definitions.

content.thinking:
The issue is that the chart components have non-standard types that TypeScript can't infer as valid React components. The `ChartComponentType` interface is a callable function, not a standard `React.ComponentType`. When `preview.meta({ component: BarChart })` is called, the TS type system can't infer the args from `ChartComponentType`.

The fix is to not pass `component` for chart stories since the types don't match, and only use `render`. But the meta requires understanding what the component type is.

Actually, looking more carefully at the error, it says `Property 'args' is missing in type` and `args: never`. 

The type system is treating `args` as `never` because the chart components' generic type doesn't align with what `preview.meta` expects for `component` — when it can't extract props, it defaults to an impossible type. I should try removing `component` from the chart story metas entirely, or I might need to check if the meta function actually requires it.

Looking at the type signature, `component` is optional, so omitting it shouldn't break anything. The render functions are already explicitly creating the components anyway. Let me check what errors the other stories like Button, Header, and Page are hitting.

which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta
